title: How to rebuild trust in other people after repeated betrayal
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

htmlContent:
Trust is not a light switch you flip back on

I know what you're thinking. You want someone to give you a formula. A step by step guide. A checklist that, if you follow it perfectly, will make trust feel safe again.

I've been there too. After enough betrayals, your nervous system stops believing in the possibility of safe people. Every new face becomes a potential threat. Every kind gesture gets scanned for hidden motives. And honestly, that's not a character flaw. It's a survival adaptation. Your body learned something real. It learned that people can hurt you, and that your trust can be weaponized against you.

Here's the thing. Rebuilding trust after repeated betrayal isn't about finding the "right" people or learning to be more optimistic. It's about rewiring a nervous system that has been trained, through lived experience, to expect danger from other humans.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, would tell you that your body's protective responses aren't irrational. They're neuroceptive. Your nervous system scans for cues of safety and danger below your conscious awareness. And when you've been betrayed repeatedly, your neuroception gets calibrated to detect threats everywhere. Even in places where they don't exist. Even in people who are actually safe.

So let's be honest about what we're working with here. Your trust isn't broken because you're naive or weak. It's broken because your body is doing exactly what it evolved to do. Protect you from harm. The problem is that this protection system, once calibrated for betrayal, can keep you trapped in isolation long after the actual danger has passed.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery by Judith Herman. In it, she describes how trauma destroys the basic sense of connection to other people. That's what we're dealing with here. Not just a bruised ego. A fundamental rupture in your ability to feel safe with others.

The trap of total withdrawal

I tried the isolation route. For years. I told myself I was being smart. I told myself I was protecting my peace. I built walls so high and so thick that nothing could get in. No one could hurt me if I never let anyone close.

And you know what happened? I got safe. I also got lonely. I got depressed. I got stuck in a loop where every interaction confirmed my belief that people couldn't be trusted, because I never gave anyone the chance to prove otherwise.

Think about it. Your brain has a confirmation bias. If you believe people are dangerous, you will find evidence everywhere. The friend who cancels plans. The partner who forgets to text back. The coworker who seems nice but you just know they're fake. Your brain will collect these data points and use them to build an airtight case for permanent isolation.

But here's what I've learned from working with my own nervous system and from studying people like Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing. The answer isn't to force yourself to trust everyone. That's toxic positivity dressed up as healing. The answer is to slowly, carefully, and on your own terms, teach your nervous system that some people are actually safe.

This is not a cognitive exercise. You cannot think your way into trusting people. Your prefrontal cortex can tell you all day that someone is trustworthy, but if your vagus nerve is screaming danger, you will feel unsafe. Period. The body leads. The mind follows.

Start with small, reversible experiments

I'm going to suggest something that might feel uncomfortable. Ready? Don't try to trust anyone. Instead, run small experiments.

An experiment is different from a commitment. A commitment says "I trust you completely and I'm all in." An experiment says "I'm going to share this one small thing and see what happens."

Maybe you tell a friend that you had a hard day. Not the whole story. Just that one sentence. Then you watch. Do they dismiss it? Do they try to fix it? Do they actually hear you? Or do they change the subject to themselves?

Maybe you ask someone for a small favor. Borrow a book. Ask for a ride. Request feedback on something trivial. Then you observe. Do they follow through? Do they respect your boundaries? Do they make you feel like a burden?

Stay with me here. These experiments are not about testing other people to catch them failing. They're about giving your nervous system new data. Your body needs to see, over and over, that some interactions end safely. That some people can be counted on for small things before you ever consider trusting them with big things.

This is slow work. I'm talking months, not weeks. Years, not months. But your nervous system didn't learn to distrust people overnight. It won't unlearn that overnight either.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts by Richard Schwartz. His Internal Family Systems model helps you understand that the part of you that doesn't trust anyone isn't your enemy. It's a protector. A part that took on a difficult job to keep you safe. Learning to listen to that part, rather than override it, changes everything.

Learn to trust yourself first

Here's something nobody tells you about betrayal. The deepest wound isn't that you can't trust other people. It's that you can't trust yourself.

You look back at every relationship that went wrong and you ask yourself: How did I miss the signs? Why did I ignore my gut? How could I let this happen again?

And those questions, while understandable, are also cruel. They assume you had full information and chose to ignore it. But that's not how betrayal works. Betrayal works because someone hides their true intentions. Because they say one thing and do another. Because they manipulate your perception of reality until you don't know which way is up.

So rebuilding trust in others actually starts with rebuilding trust in yourself. With your own perceptions. Your own boundaries. Your own ability to say no and mean it.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook by Arielle Schwartz. It walks you through rebuilding that internal foundation. The one that says: I will believe myself. I will honor my own limits. I will leave when I need to leave.

When you trust yourself to walk away from anyone who betrays you, you can actually take risks with people. Because the stakes are lower. You know you'll be okay even if it doesn't work out. You have your own back.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did something relax? Or did something tighten? Both responses are valid. Both responses are information.

Discernment is not judgment

There's a difference between judging people harshly and discerning whether they're safe for you. Judgment says "this person is bad." Discernment says "this person is not a good fit for my nervous system right now."

I had to learn this distinction the hard way. I spent years in a binary mindset. Either someone was completely safe or they were completely dangerous. Either I trusted them fully or I kept them at arm's length forever. There was no middle ground.

But human relationships are not binary. They're gradient. Someone can be safe enough to have coffee with but not safe enough to share your deepest wounds with. Someone can be trustworthy in one context and unreliable in another. People are complex, and your trust can be complex too.

Dan Siegel, the psychiatrist who developed interpersonal neurobiology, talks about integration. The ability to hold different parts of experience together without splitting. You can hold that someone hurt you and that someone also has good qualities. You can hold that you care about someone and that you need distance from them. Both things can be true at the same time.

This kind of thinking is hard for a traumatized nervous system. Splitting is easier. All good or all bad. All safe or all dangerous. But integration is where real healing happens. It's where you get to have relationships with flawed human beings, including yourself.

Right? Think about what that would mean for your life. The freedom to connect with people without having to decide if they're completely trustworthy or completely not. The freedom to take people as they are, with their limitations and their gifts.

The slow return to connection

I'm not going to tell you that you'll eventually trust everyone. That's not realistic, and it's not even desirable. Some people are genuinely not safe. Some situations genuinely warrant caution. Your nervous system's protective instincts are not the enemy. They're just sometimes overgeneralized.

The goal isn't to become naive again. The goal is to become discerning. To have your protective system calibrated accurately rather than hyper-vigilantly. To be able to sense real danger while also allowing safe connection.

This takes time. It takes practice. It takes letting yourself be disappointed without letting that disappointment become proof that all people are the same.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents by Lindsay Gibson. It helps explain why you learned to distrust in the first place. Why your expectations for relationships got shaped by people who couldn't meet them. Understanding that context doesn't excuse the betrayals, but it does help you stop taking them so personally.

And here's the part that's hard to hear. Some people will betray you again. That's the risk of being alive and connected. There are no guarantees. But the alternative is to live behind walls so high that nothing can reach you, including love, including joy, including the kind of connection that makes life worth living.

I don't know where you are in this process. Maybe you're still in the isolation phase, and that's where you need to be right now. Maybe you're starting to crack the door open and peek outside. Maybe you've already taken some small risks and gotten burned, and you're wondering if it's worth trying again.

All of those places are valid. All of them are part of the return.

The return to trust is not a straight line. It's a spiral. You'll circle back to the same fears, the same protective responses, the same questions. But each time you circle back, you'll be a little more aware. A little more able to hold your own hand through the fear. A little more willing to let someone else hold it too.

That's the work. That's the return. And it's worth every painful, awkward, terrifying step.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Trauma and Recovery by Judith Herman - A foundational text on understanding trauma and the path to reconnection
  • No Bad Parts by Richard Schwartz - A gentle introduction to Internal Family Systems and working with protective parts
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook by Arielle Schwartz - A practical workbook for rebuilding trust in yourself and others
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents by Lindsay Gibson - For understanding how early relationships shaped your trust patterns
